Farn Por-rrrcal PnncrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
January 8,2003
Patricia Lambert
4268 5 Hathaway Crossing
Point Aren4 CA95468
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-349
Dear Ms. Lambert:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').l Please note that the
Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulations
18329(bX8XA) a"d (c)(a)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, any conclusions contained
herein apply only to prospective actions. Please also note that other conflict-of-interest
laws outside the jurisdiction of the Act might apply to you.
QUESTTON
What conflicts of interest might arise by virtue of the fact that your son was
recently hired as administrator for the board on which you sit as a member?
CONCLUSION
A public official cannot participate in a governmental decision that has either a
personal financial effect on the official, or on a member of his or her immediate faniily,
or has a material financial effect on a source of income to the official. Barring such an
effect on your personal financial interests, the Act does not prohibit you from
participating in a governmental decision merelybecause the decision will financially
affect your adult child.
I Government Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
t:


File No. I-02-349
Page No. 2
You are a member of the coast Life Support Dishict Board of Directors
('CLSD')' CLSD is a rural ambulance districiserving the southem coastal region of
Mendocino county and the northern coastal region ofionoma counfy. your son has just
been hired as the new administrator of CLSD.
F'ACTS
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits financial conflicts of interest. Section gTl11provides that no
public official may "make," "participate in making,,, or in any *"y i[ilptto use his or
her official position to "influence" a govemmentaidecision in whlch the imciat knows,
or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. The Commission has
{9vetog9a an eight-step approach for determiung wtret}rer an individ; h; "
disqualifring financial interest in a decision. @elulation 1g700(b;.y necause we have
been presented with neither a Sovenrmental decis--ion nor a financial interest, two crucial
steps in the conflict-of-interest analysis, we cannot do more than offerg*oa advice.
Step One: Are you a *public fficial"?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to ,,public officials.,,
(sections 87100, B7r03; regulation tszooqulr).)^A:.p,ruti" official,,is defined as a
"member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local gor"*rot-ug;.v. . . .,,
(S-ection 82048') "Membet'' is further defined to include "salaried or unsalaried members
of committees, boards or commisiions with decisionmaking agthority.- (Reg. ts70l(a).)
As a member of the CLSD, we assume that you are a public official.
step Two: Ar1)t-ou "making," "parttcipattng in making,, or ,,tnfluencing,, a
goyernmental decision?
The Act's confl-ict-oiinterest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in maki.rg ot in yy yuy iti"irrptf.l to use his official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or t u. reason to know he has a
financial interest-" (Section g7100; regulation 1g700@xa.) A public officiar ,?nakes a
governmental decision" when the official, acting within ifre autnorty of his or her office
or position, votes on amatter, obligates or commits his or t o ug"""y to *y .oorr" or
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of hiJor {", ,g;";.
(Regulation 18702.1.) A public officiil "participates in a governmental decision,, when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recoflrmendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmentar.decision. (Regulation I g7oii.; ;pubric
official is attempting to use his or her official position to i"rrfl.r"n"" a decision i{ for the
purpose of influencing, the official contacts oiuppeurs before any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Iieguration 1s702.3.)


File No. I-02-349
Page No. 3
Step Three: What are your economic interests?
An official h?. l disqualifying financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have amateialfinancial effect,aistinguishable from its
gffect on the public generally, on the official, his or her immedi it" Aiiy,or on the
following enumerated economic interests :
r { public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect invesfinent of $2,000 or more 1r""tior, g7103(a);
regulation I 87 03 .l (a));
r { public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer,p^artner, trustee, emproyee, or holds"any position of
management (section g 7 I 03 (d); regulation I g703. I (b));
r I public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section g7103(b);
regulation 18703.2);
r { public official has an economic interest in any source of incomg including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior"
to the decision (section 97r03(c);reguration rg703.3);
r I public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months priJr to the decision
(section 87 I 03 (e); regularion tg7 03.4)2 ;
r { public ofificial has an economic interest in his or herpersonal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immeaiate family.
This isknow-n as the "personal financial effects" rule (section g7103,
regulation I8703.5). An official's "immediate family"'means the official,s
spouse and dependent children. (Section g2}2g)
You have notidentified any economic interests that will lead to a conflict of
interest for you. We have no facts to indicate that your son is a.,source of income,, to
you (section 87103(c)) and therefore we cannot further analyzepotential conflicts of
interest. Some examples of ways in which your son could be considered to be a source of
income to you are if he provides you financial support or if, perhaps, there are
outstanding debts he owes to you. A conflict of interest rorld also arise if a decision
regarding your son would somehow inctease or decrease your expenses enough to meet
the personal financial effect,threshold ($250 in any l2-monthperiod). ls"e rigotatio.,
18703-5 which defines an effect on "personal finances,, as any effeci where the
"decisions will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official
' Your son will not be a "source ofa gift,, forp,,rposes of the Act. (Section g7103(e).) The definifion of
"gift" excludes gifts from an individuals chitd. (section s202g(bX3).)


File No. I_02_349
Page No. 4
or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.,,3) Barring afinancial
relationship between you and your son, you will not harve a conflict of interest in a
decision solely because the decisiofimay have a financialeffect on you6o* rh"
familial relationship between you and your son does not, by itsel{, ir"ut" i ionflict of
interest for you. (OvromAdvice Letter,No. A_02_254.)
Steps 4 - 8.
We have not gone on to describe the last five steps because we have no
information to suggest you have an economic interest in your son nor do we have
information regarding aprospective decision in which you might be involved. However,
we have enclosed apamphlet entitled, o'Can I Vote?" "*ptui"iig the entire eigftt-step
process for your information.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

QUESTION: What conflicts of interest might arise by virtue of the fact that your son was
recently hired as administrator for the board on which you sit as a member?

CONCLUSION: A public official cannot participate in a governmental decision that has either a
personal financial effect on the official, or on a member of his or her immediate faniily,
or has a material financial effect on a source of income to the official. Barring such an
effect on your personal financial interests, the Act does not prohibit you from
participating in a governmental decision merelybecause the decision will financially
affect your adult child.
